current_pain_and_therapy_goals.what_eases_your_pain:
Applying heat to my lower back (either a heating pad or a warm bath) tends to ease the stiffness and dull ache quite a bit, especially in the evenings after work. Gentle stretching routines that my primary care doctor printed out for me (mostly hamstring, hip flexor, and lower back stretches) also help if I do them consistently, usually first thing in the morning and again before bed. Over-the-counter ibuprofen 400–600 mg reduces the intensity of the pain enough that I can get through my workday without constantly shifting around in my chair.

Changing positions frequently—alternating between sitting, standing, and short walks around the office—helps prevent the pain from building up. I recently bought an adjustable standing desk, and being able to stand for part of the day makes a noticeable difference. When I’m at home, lying on my back with a pillow under my knees or lying on my side with a pillow between my knees eases the pressure in my lower back and down my right leg.

Emotionally, keeping my stress levels down helps too. On days when I take the time to do a short guided meditation (10–15 minutes using an app) or some deep breathing, I notice I tense my muscles less, and that seems to reduce the pain. Light activity like slow walking on flat ground (for example, walking my dog around the block) is usually okay and sometimes even makes me feel looser afterward, as long as I don’t overdo it. Overall, gentle movement, heat, good posture, and pacing my activities seem to be the biggest things that ease my pain.
current_pain_and_therapy_goals.what_makes_your_pain_worse:
Prolonged sitting is the biggest trigger, especially sitting at my computer for more than 30–45 minutes at a time. I work as an office manager and spend a lot of time at a desk, and if I get absorbed in a task and forget to stand up, I can feel the pain creeping in across my lower back and then radiating down my right buttock and into the back of my thigh. Long car rides are particularly bad—anything over an hour leaves me very stiff and sore when I get out of the car.

Lifting and carrying heavier items also aggravates the pain. For example, carrying multiple grocery bags from the car to the kitchen or lifting the large box of printer paper at work will often cause a sharp twinge in my lower back. Bending forward repeatedly—like when I’m doing laundry, loading the dishwasher, or weeding in the garden—tends to make the pain flare up later in the day. I’ve noticed that twisting motions, such as turning quickly to grab something behind me or twisting while lifting, almost always result in a spike of pain.

High-impact activities like jogging or anything that involves jumping are basically off-limits now because they cause a deep, throbbing pain that can last into the next day. Even walking on uneven ground, like on hiking trails, can aggravate the pain if I’m not careful. Sleeping on a soft mattress or falling asleep on the couch also makes me wake up with more intense stiffness and pain the next morning.

Stress and fatigue seem to make everything worse as well. During particularly busy weeks at work or when I don’t sleep well (which happens a few nights a week due to discomfort), my pain threshold seems lower and even normal daily activities like cooking dinner, standing to fold laundry, or sitting through my son’s basketball games become difficult. Cold, damp weather also appears to make my back feel tighter and more uncomfortable, although I’m not sure if that’s just in my head or actually related.
current_pain_and_therapy_goals.what_are_your_goals_in_physical_therapy:
My main goal in physical therapy is to reduce my chronic lower back and right leg pain enough that I can comfortably do my daily activities and keep working full time without constantly worrying about my back. I would love to be able to sit through a 60–90 minute meeting at work and drive for an hour without feeling that deep ache and radiating pain down my right leg afterward. I’d also like to learn specific exercises and stretches that I can safely do on my own at home to maintain any improvements I make during therapy, so I’m not just dependent on appointments.

Functionally, I want to get back to being able to walk 2–3 miles on flat ground at a moderate pace without having to stop because of back or leg pain. Before this started, I used to walk regularly with a friend after work a couple of times a week, and I’d really like to return to that routine. I also want to be able to do basic chores—like vacuuming, laundry, and grocery shopping—without having to break them up into tiny segments or pay for them later with a pain flare-up.

I’m hoping physical therapy can help me improve my core strength, posture, and body mechanics so that I can protect my back long-term. I’d like to understand what movements or positions are safe versus which ones I should modify or avoid. Education is important to me—I want to know what’s actually going on with my spine and muscles, and what I can realistically expect over the next 6–12 months.

On a more personal level, I want to be able to be more active with my family. I have a 13-year-old son and a 10-year-old daughter, and it’s hard when I have to say no to things like going on longer walks, light hikes, or playing outside because I’m worried about my back. If physical therapy can help me feel more confident and capable physically, that would mean a lot. Ultimately, my goal is not necessarily to be completely pain-free (I understand that might not be realistic), but to have the pain reduced and better controlled so it doesn’t dictate what I can and cannot do every day.

previous_treatment_and_diagnostic_tests.other_previous_treatment_type: Saw my primary care physician (Dr. Angela Ruiz) in March 2025. She prescribed a short course of prescription-strength ibuprofen and recommended basic home stretching exercises. I also had 4 chiropractic sessions between April and May 2025 focusing on spinal adjustments and some basic traction. The chiropractic care provided temporary relief for a few days after each session, but the pain kept returning, especially with prolonged sitting at work. I have not had formal physical therapy for this specific issue before; this referral is my first time starting structured PT.

past_and_current_medical_conditions.explain_yes_and_additional_conditions:
Allergies: Seasonal environmental allergies (pollen, grass, and dust mites). Symptoms include sneezing, itchy/watery eyes, and nasal congestion, especially in the spring and early fall. I take cetirizine 10 mg daily and use a nasal steroid spray as needed, which usually keeps symptoms under control. No known medication allergies.

Anxiety: Diagnosed with generalized anxiety disorder in 2018. I am currently stable on sertraline 50 mg daily, prescribed by my primary care physician. I also see a therapist about once a month for ongoing support. My anxiety tends to manifest as excessive worrying (especially about work, my kids, and my health) and occasional restlessness and difficulty sleeping. I have not had panic attacks in several years. My mental health is generally well-managed but can flare during periods of high stress or when my pain is worse.

Depression: History of mild to moderate depression, first diagnosed around the same time as my anxiety in 2018. It is managed with the same medication (sertraline) and therapy. Symptoms in the past have included low mood, fatigue, and loss of interest in activities I usually enjoy. Over the past year, my mood has been mostly stable, but the chronic back pain does sometimes make me feel discouraged and frustrated.

Fractures: I fractured my left wrist (distal radius) in 2012 after slipping on ice in a parking lot. It was treated with a cast for 6 weeks, followed by a short course of physical therapy. I regained full function and do not have ongoing issues with that wrist. No other significant fractures.

High cholesterol: Diagnosed with hyperlipidemia in 2021 after routine blood work. I was started on atorvastatin 20 mg nightly. My most recent labs (February 2025) showed improvement in my cholesterol numbers, and my doctor advised continuing the medication along with diet and exercise changes.

Headaches: I experience tension-type headaches about 1–2 times per week, usually later in the day after long periods of computer work or when I’m stressed. The pain is typically a band-like pressure around my forehead and the back of my head. Over-the-counter ibuprofen and rest usually relieve them. I very rarely get migraines (maybe once or twice a year), which involve one-sided throbbing pain and light sensitivity; for those, I lie down in a dark room and take ibuprofen.

Vision problems: I am nearsighted and have astigmatism. I wear glasses or contact lenses full time. My last eye exam was in late 2024, and my prescription was updated then. I do not have glaucoma, macular degeneration, or diabetic eye disease.

Additional conditions/precautions: I sometimes have mild lower back stiffness even on days when the pain is not severe, so I try to avoid heavy lifting and sudden twisting motions. No history of blood clots, stroke, or heart attack. No history of surgery requiring metal implants. No known issues with anesthesia. I do not smoke, drink alcohol only socially (1–2 drinks per month at most), and do not use recreational drugs.

current_medications.reason_taking_row_1: Management of generalized anxiety disorder and mild recurrent depression; helps reduce excessive worrying, improves mood stability, and decreases physical symptoms of anxiety.
current_medications.reason_taking_row_2: Treatment of high cholesterol (hyperlipidemia) to reduce LDL cholesterol and lower long-term cardiovascular risk.
current_medications.frequency_row_3: Once daily as needed, usually during allergy seasons
current_medications.reason_taking_row_3: Relief of seasonal allergy symptoms including sneezing, itchy/watery eyes, and nasal congestion due to pollen and dust mites.
current_medications.frequency_row_4: Up to 3 times daily as needed for pain; typically 3–4 days per week
current_medications.reason_taking_row_4: Management of lower back pain and occasional tension headaches; taken with food to reduce stomach upset.
